questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the IUPAC name for the simplest alkyne with the formula \(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\)?
questions.hint: The suffix '-yne' indicates a triple bond.
questions.explanation: Ethyne is the IUPAC name for \(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\), containing a carbon-carbon triple bond. Option D, acetylene, is the common name, while B and C are alkenes/alkanes.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Ethyne is the IUPAC name for the alkyne with two carbon atoms and a triple bond.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The parent alkane 'ethane' (-ane) is changed to 'ethyne' (-yne) to indicate the \(\mathrm{C≡C}\) triple bond.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Eth + yne = Ethyne. The 'yne' tells us there is a triple bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For alkynes, the suffix '-yne' replaces '-ane'. Thus, \(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\) is named ethyne.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Ethyne (\(\mathrm{HC≡CH}\)) is the first member of the alkyne homologous series. Its common name, acetylene, is still widely used in industry.
reasoning_steps: Identify the number of carbons (2).
Identify the functional group (triple bond).
Apply IUPAC suffix '-yne' to root 'eth'.
misconceptions: Confusing common name (acetylene) with IUPAC name (ethyne).

glossary_entry.definition: The IUPAC name for acetylene, the simplest alkyne with formula \(\mathrm{HC≡CH}\).

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Ethyne is the IUPAC name.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the IUPAC name for the compound \(\mathrm{CH≡C-CH_2-CH_3}\)?
questions.hint: Number the chain to give the triple bond the lowest number.
questions.explanation: The longest chain containing the triple bond is four carbons (butyne). Numbering from the right places the triple bond at C1, giving 1-butyne. Option B would have the triple bond between C2 and C3.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: 1-Butyne is a four-carbon alkyne with the triple bond at the end of the chain.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The parent chain is four carbons (butane → butyne). Numbering gives the triple bond the lowest number, so the \(\mathrm{≡CH}\) group is at C1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Count the carbons (4). The triple bond is at the end, so it's 1-butyne.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Structure: \(\mathrm{HC≡C-CH_2-CH_3}\). Numbering from right: C1 has the triple bond. Correct name: but-1-yne or 1-butyne.
explanations_tiered.advanced: IUPAC rules prioritize the triple bond for numbering over alkyl substituents. The locant for the triple bond should be as low as possible.
reasoning_steps: Find the longest chain containing the triple bond (4 C).
Number from the end nearest the triple bond (starts at C1).
Name: 1-butyne.
misconceptions: Numbering from the wrong end, giving 3-butyne (incorrect).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Terminal alkyne = triple bond at C1.

glossary_entry.definition: An alkyne with the triple bond at the end of the carbon chain (\(\mathrm{≡CH}\)).

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An alkyne with the triple bond at the chain end.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use IUPAC rules to name simple alkynes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Triple bond at C1 gives 1-butyne.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What type of isomerism is exhibited by 1-butyne and 2-butyne?
questions.hint: Both have the same carbon skeleton but differ in the location of the triple bond.
questions.explanation: Position isomerism occurs when the functional group (triple bond) is at a different position on the carbon chain. Option A (chain) would involve different carbon skeletons, which these do not.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: 1- butyne and 2- butyne are position isomers

explanations_tiered.definition: Position isomerism occurs when the same functional group is located at different positions on the carbon chain.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Both 1-butyne and 2-butyne have the formula \(\mathrm{C_4H_6}\) and a four-carbon chain, but the triple bond is at C1 in one and C2 in the other.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Same skeleton, different spot for the triple bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: 1-Butyne: \(\mathrm{HC≡C-CH_2-CH_3}\). 2-Butyne: \(\mathrm{CH_3-C≡C-CH_3}\). The skeleton is the same (4 carbons straight), only the triple bond position differs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Position isomerism is a subclass of structural isomerism. Alkynes cannot exhibit cis-trans isomerism because the triple bond is linear, restricting substituents to 180° apart.
reasoning_steps: Compare the carbon skeletons (both are straight chains).
Compare the position of the triple bond (C1 vs C2).
Recognize this as position isomerism.
misconceptions: Assuming alkynes can have cis-trans isomerism (they cannot).

glossary_entry.definition: Isomerism where the same functional group is located at different positions on the carbon chain.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is 1-butyne and 2-butyne chain or position isomers?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Position isomers (same chain, different C≡C location).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Isomers differing by the location of a functional group.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Alkynes have linear geometry, no cis/trans.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Which of the following pairs are chain isomers?
questions.hint: Chain isomers have different carbon skeletons (branching).
questions.explanation: 1-Pentyne (straight chain) and 3-methyl-1-butyne (branched) have the same formula \(\mathrm{C_5H_8}\) but different carbon arrangements. Option A are position isomers, B and D have different formulas.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: 3- methyl- 1- butyne and 1- pentyne are chain isomers

explanations_tiered.definition: Chain isomers have the same molecular formula but different branching patterns of the carbon skeleton.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Both have 5 carbons and a triple bond, but 1-pentyne is linear while 3-methyl-1-butyne has a methyl branch on the third carbon.
explanations_tiered.beginner: One is a straight chain, the other has a branch off the side.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Chain isomerism is also known as skeletal isomerism. For alkynes, this occurs when the carbon chain arrangement changes while the triple bond position remains the same or changes accordingly.
reasoning_steps: Check if the molecular formulas are identical.
Compare the carbon skeletons (straight vs branched).
Identify the pair with different skeletons.
misconceptions: Confusing chain isomerism with position isomerism.

glossary_entry.definition: Isomerism resulting from different arrangements of the carbon chain (branching).

flashcard.flashcard_back: Yes, one is straight, one is branched.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Same formula, different carbon skeleton branching.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Different skeletons (straight vs branched).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Ethyne (\(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\)) is commonly prepared in the laboratory by reacting calcium carbide (\(\mathrm{CaC_2}\)) with which compound?
questions.hint: This reaction produces a metal hydroxide and the alkyne gas.
questions.explanation: Calcium carbide reacts with water: \(\mathrm{CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Ca(OH)_2 + C_2H_2}\). Option A would also react but produces side products; water is the standard lab reagent.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: Reaction of calcium carbide with water

explanations_tiered.definition: Calcium carbide reacts with water to produce ethyne via a hydrolysis reaction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The carbide ion (\(\mathrm{C_2^{2-}}\)) reacts with water protons to form the neutral alkyne, \(\mathrm{HC≡CH}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Drip water on calcium carbide rocks to make acetylene gas.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The reaction is \(\mathrm{CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Ca(OH)_2 + C_2H_2}\). The gas is collected over water.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is a classic preparation of acetylene. The reaction is vigorous and exothermic. Calcium carbide is produced industrially from lime and coke in an electric furnace.
reasoning_steps: Recall the formula of calcium carbide (CaC2).
Identify that it reacts with water to form Ca(OH)2 and C2H2.
Select water as the reagent.
misconceptions: Thinking acid is needed (though acid works, water is the standard lab method).

glossary_entry.definition: A chemical compound \(\mathrm{CaC_2}\) that reacts with water to produce acetylene.

flashcard.front: How is ethyne prepared from calcium carbide?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Lab prep of ethyne from CaC2?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method to produce alkynes from metal carbides and water.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Prepare acetylene from CaC2 and water
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Water is the standard reagent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: In the lab preparation of ethyne from calcium carbide, why is the gas collected over water?

options.C: Ethyne is insoluble in water and less dense than water
options.D: To remove impurities of hydrogen sulfide

questions.hint: Ethyne is nonpolar and does not dissolve well in water.
questions.explanation: Ethyne is insoluble in water and does not react with it, allowing collection via downward displacement of water. Option A is false; it is only slightly soluble. Option D refers to a specific purification step.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: collect several jars of the gas over water

explanations_tiered.definition: Water displacement is used for gases that are insoluble in water and do not react with it.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ethyne is nonpolar and insoluble in water, so it bubbles through water without dissolving, allowing collection in a gas jar.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Ethyne doesn't mix with water, so it pushes the water out of the jar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Because ethyne has low solubility in water (unlike ammonia or HCl), it can be trapped by inverting a gas jar over the delivery tube.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solubility of ethyne in water is approximately 1 g/L. This is low enough for efficient collection via pneumatic trough, though some is lost to dissolution.
reasoning_steps: Recall the polarity of ethyne (nonpolar).
Recall that nonpolar gases do not dissolve well in polar water.
Conclude it can be collected over water.
misconceptions: Thinking it is collected over water because it is heavy (it is slightly lighter than air).

glossary_entry.definition: A method to collect gases that are insoluble in water.

flashcard.front: Why is ethyne collected over water?
flashcard.flashcard_back: It is insoluble in water and does not react with it.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A device used to collect gases over water.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Insolubility allows water displacement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: A student tries to prepare ethyne using the reaction of calcium carbide with water but observes that the gas produces a yellow flame and smells like garlic. What is the most likely reason?

options.C: Impurities such as phosphine (\(\mathrm{PH_3}\)) and arsine (\(\mathrm{AsH_3}\)) are present
options.D: The gas is methane, not ethyne

questions.hint: Commercial calcium carbide often contains impurities like calcium phosphide.
questions.explanation: Technical grade calcium carbide contains \(\mathrm{Ca_3P_2}\) and \(\mathrm{Ca_3As_2}\), which react with water to form toxic, smelly gases (\(\mathrm{PH_3}\), \(\mathrm{AsH_3}\)) that cause the characteristic odor and smoky flame. Option A would produce pure, odorless ethyne.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Impurities in calcium carbide produce toxic phosphine and arsine, giving a garlic smell.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Calcium phosphide (\(\mathrm{Ca_3P_2}\)) reacts with water to form phosphine (\(\mathrm{PH_3}\)), which is spontaneously flammable and smells like garlic.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Dirty rocks make stinky, smoky gas; clean ones make clean gas.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pure \(\mathrm{CaC_2}\) yields pure \(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\) (colorless, odorless, non-toxic). Impurities cause the 'carbide smell' and yellow sooty flame due to \(\mathrm{PH_3}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Phosphine (\(\mathrm{PH_3}\)) is a reducing agent and pyrophoric. Its presence leads to immediate ignition of the gas jet, causing a smoky flame unlike the clean blue flame of pure acetylene.
reasoning_steps: Recognize the smell and yellow flame as abnormal.
Recall that commercial CaC2 has impurities.
Identify the impurities (Ca3P2, Ca3As2).
misconceptions: Assuming pure ethyne has a garlic smell (it does not).

glossary_entry.definition: A toxic, flammable gas (\(\mathrm{PH_3}\)) with a garlic-like odor, often present as an impurity in carbide-derived ethyne.

flashcard.front: Why does lab ethyne smell like garlic?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Due to phosphine (\(\mathrm{PH_3}\)) impurities from calcium phosphide.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Garlic smell in ethyne prep indicates?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A toxic gas produced from calcium phosphide impurities.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain purification by passing through CuSO4 solution.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Impurities cause the odor and smoky flame.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Why does ethyne burn with a smoky, luminous flame compared to the clean blue flame of methane?

options.B: Ethyne has a higher carbon-to-hydrogen ratio, leading to incomplete combustion
options.D: Ethyne requires more oxygen to burn completely

questions.hint: The carbon content relative to hydrogen affects soot production.
questions.explanation: Ethyne (\(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\)) has a 1:1 C:H ratio, while methane (\(\mathrm{CH_4}\)) has 1:4. The higher carbon ratio causes incomplete combustion producing carbon particles (soot). Option D is true but describes the cause of incomplete combustion.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: Alkynes burn with a smoky luminous flame due to their high carbon content

explanations_tiered.definition: The carbon-to-hydrogen ratio influences combustion completeness; higher C:H ratio yields more soot.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ethyne has insufficient oxygen for complete combustion in air, leading to carbon particle formation (soot) which glows yellow.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Complete combustion: \(\mathrm{2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O}\). In limited air, \(\mathrm{C_2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2C + H_2O + CO}\). The hot carbon glows yellow.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The high percentage of carbon (92.3% by mass in ethyne vs 75% in methane) requires precise stoichiometry to avoid soot. In an oxy-acetylene torch, pure oxygen ensures complete combustion, producing a very hot blue flame.
reasoning_steps: Compare the C:H ratio of ethyne (1:1) and methane (1:4).
Recognize that high carbon content leads to incomplete combustion.
Identify that carbon particles cause the smoky flame.
misconceptions: Thinking the flame color is due to the triple bond itself.

glossary_entry.definition: A yellow, luminous flame resulting from incomplete combustion, indicating high carbon content.

flashcard.front: Why does ethyne burn with a smoky flame?
flashcard.flashcard_back: High carbon-to-hydrogen ratio causes incomplete combustion, producing soot.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: High carbon content causes incomplete combustion.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Combustion that occurs with insufficient oxygen, producing carbon monoxide or carbon soot.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. High carbon ratio causes soot.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the product when ethyne undergoes complete hydrogenation in the presence of a nickel catalyst?
questions.hint: Complete hydrogenation adds four hydrogen atoms to ethyne.
questions.explanation: Complete hydrogenation of a triple bond yields an alkane: \(\mathrm{HC≡CH + 2H_2 \xrightarrow{Ni} CH_3CH_3}\). Option A would be partial hydrogenation (using Lindlar's catalyst).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Hydrogenation adds molecular hydrogen to a multiple bond, converting alkynes to alkanes under complete reduction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The triple bond requires 2 moles of \(\mathrm{H_2}\) to fully saturate, breaking both the \(\pi\) bonds to form a single C-C bond with four C-H bonds.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reaction: \(\mathrm{HC≡CH + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3-CH_3}\). Nickel or platinum catalysts are used.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hydrogenation proceeds in two steps: alkyne → alkene (requires 1 mol H2) then alkene → alkane (requires another mol H2). Palladium catalysts can be poisoned (Lindlar's) to stop at the alkene.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that complete hydrogenation breaks all multiple bonds.
Add 2 H2 molecules to C2H2.
Result is C2H6 (ethane).
misconceptions: Thinking alkynes only add one H2 (giving alkene).

glossary_entry.definition: Addition of hydrogen to all multiple bonds in a molecule, yielding a saturated product.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Addition of hydrogen to saturate all bonds.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Complete addition yields ethane.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.sub_subsection: E. Properties and uses of Acetylene or Ethyne
questions.structural_anchor: E. Properties and uses of Acetylene or Ethyne
questions.question: Which application utilizes the extremely high flame temperature (approximately \(3000^\circ \text{C}\)) produced by burning ethyne in pure oxygen?
questions.hint: The intense heat is used to melt and join metals.
questions.explanation: Oxy-acetylene welding uses the high-temperature flame to cut and weld metals. Options A, B, and D are chemical or agricultural uses that do not rely on the combustion temperature.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.structural_element: E. Properties and uses of Acetylene or Ethyne
source_trace.content_anchor: oxy- acetylene torches for cutting and welding metals

explanations_tiered.definition: Oxy-acetylene welding is a process that uses a flame of ethyne and oxygen to melt metals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The combustion of ethyne in pure oxygen produces a flame hot enough (~3000°C) to melt steel, allowing metal pieces to be joined.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Acetylene + Oxygen = Super hot fire for cutting metal.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reaction: \(\mathrm{2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O}\) releases immense heat, focused in a torch.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The high flame temperature results from the high bond energy of \(\mathrm{C≡C}\) and the exothermicity of the reaction. It is one of the hottest flames achievable with common industrial gases.
reasoning_steps: Recall the high temperature property of ethyne combustion.
Match this property to an application requiring extreme heat.
Select welding/cutting.
misconceptions: Using ethyne for ripening (does not require heat).

glossary_entry.definition: A welding process using a flame of acetylene and oxygen to melt metals.

flashcard.front: What uses the high temperature of ethyne combustion?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Application of ethyne's high temp flame?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A tool that burns acetylene with oxygen to produce a high-temperature flame.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Welding utilizes the high flame temperature.
